title: China's Steel Production Continues to Grow, Air Pollution Recently Up Too
section_path: China's Steel Production Continues to Grow, Air Pollution Recently Up Too
section_path_text: China's Steel Production Continues to Grow, Air Pollution Recently Up Too
summary:
Main
By Jeffrey Landsberg
As we discussed in Commodore Research's most recent Weekly Executive Report and Weekly China Report, air pollution levels in Beijing (which is where air pollution is usually the highest due to its close proximity to China's major steel hub) increased last week. Friday saw the highest air pollution level, with PM 2.5 averaging 162. Image reference: Chart 1.jpg | assets/2025-03-07_chinas-steel-production-continues-to-grow-air-pollution-recently-up-too_img_chart1_a0d67d045670.jpg
Also of note is that the most recent data shows China's steel output at large and medium-sized mills averaged 2.15 million tons during February 11 - February 20.

PM 2.5 is the measure of hazardous particulates in the air < 2.5 micrometers in diameter. 500+
is considered "hazardous", 300-499 is considered "very unhealthy", 200-299 is considered "unhealthy".
